trm:term.trm:name: Layer
trm:term.trm:definition: A separate XML document representing one dimension of meaning within a specification. Each layer has its own namespace and schema. Layers are orthogonal: prose describes content, organization classifies it, relations link it, artifacts trace it to code.

trm:term.trm:name: Namespace
trm:term.trm:definition: An XML namespace URI that uniquely identifies a layer type. Each layer schema defines a target namespace (e.g., urn:clayers:prose). Namespaces enable the combined document to contain elements from all layers without name collisions.

trm:term.trm:name: Node
trm:term.trm:definition: Any element within a layer that carries an @id attribute. Nodes are the addressable units of a specification. All node IDs across all layers of a spec must be unique, forming a single ID space enforced by the index.

trm:term.trm:name: Index
trm:term.trm:definition: The file manifest that defines the boundary of a specification. Lists all layer files, declares the ID uniqueness domain, and provides the spec identifier and version. Non-index layers reference their index via the index attribute on the clayers root element.

pr:section.pr:title: Cognitive Layers
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: A layered XML format for software specifications that co-evolve with their implementations.
pr:section.pr:p: Software code is a degraded version of intent. Specifications are either implicit, scattered across disconnected documentation, or maintained in heavyweight tools that drift from reality. Code becomes the de facto spec, but code only captures how, not why.

Cognitive layers inverts this. Instead of iterating directly on code, iterate on the specification. The spec is the source of truth for software construction. Code is a produced artifact that implements the spec, and tooling can verify the mapping between them.

The format is a set of XML documents, each representing a distinct layer of meaning. Every layer has its own namespace and schema. The specification matures over time: it starts as prose and gains precision through additional semantic layers.

pr:section.pr:title: Layered Architecture
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Orthogonal layers, each adding meaning without replacing the others.
pr:section.pr:p: Each layer is a separate file for three reasons: file organization (each can be versioned and reviewed independently), context size control (an LLM updating artifact mappings does not need to load the prose layer), and independent evolution (prose can go through ten revisions while the artifact mapping stays pinned).

pr:tr.pr:td: File manifest, defines spec boundary and ID uniqueness domain
Named snapshots for temporal anchoring of artifact mappings
Typed semantic links within and across specs
Architecture Decision Records with status lifecycle and alternatives
External references, bibliography, and provenance tracking
Implementation plans with itemized steps and acceptance criteria
Machine-readable descriptions for language model consumption
Python API surface descriptions with structured signatures

pr:section.pr:p: All layers share a common ID space. Every node across all layer files must have a unique @id. The index defines this uniqueness domain. Every non-index layer declares which index it belongs to via the index attribute on the clayers root, making each file self-describing.

org:concept.org:purpose: Orientation for readers new to the clayers format.
Understanding why specifications are split into layers and how layers interact.

art:mapping.art:note: README.md captures the full motivation, thesis, and overview of the clayers format.
index.xsd defines the file manifest structure. The full layered architecture spans all schema files.
collect_nodes() collects all elements with @id attributes, which is the definition of a node.
is_index_file(), resolve_index(), and discover_spec_files() implement the index concept: detection, resolution, and manifest reading.
Layer URN constants, ALL_LAYERS array, and PREFIX_MAP implement the full set of namespace URIs and their canonical prefix bindings.
The 17 layer URN constants (SPEC through LAYER) plus COMBINED define the layer concept as a semantic namespace identifier.

spec:clayers.llm:schema: The spec schema defines the universal clayers root element. It uses xs:any namespace="##other" processContents="strict" so children from any layer namespace are accepted and validated. This enables open-world extensibility (new layers without modifying existing schemas) and cross-layer mixing (a single file can contain elements from multiple layers). Attributes: spec and version for identity (index files), index for back-referencing (non-index files).

The terminology schema defines a controlled vocabulary. Each <term> has an id, a canonical name, and a definition. Other layers reference terms by ID via <trm:ref term="..."> elements for precision. When prose says "settlement", it links to the canonical definition rather than relying on implicit understanding.

An inline reference to a defined term, used within prose content. The term attribute is the ID of the referenced term. The element content is display text, which may differ from the canonical term name for grammatical reasons (e.g., "settlements" linking to term-settlement).

The prose schema provides DITA-style technical writing elements. Content is organized into <section> elements, each with a required id and title. Sections contain paragraphs, lists, ordered steps, notes (with severity types), code blocks, tables, and cross-references. Inline content can embed terminology references and bold/italic/code markup.

A structural unit of prose content. Requires an id (for cross-referencing) and a title. Contains an optional shortdesc followed by body elements: paragraphs, lists, steps, notes, code blocks, tables, and nested sections.
A cross-reference to another node in the specification. The ref attribute is a node ID. In the combined document, this is validated via keyref to ensure the target exists.

The organization schema classifies spec nodes by topic type, inspired by DITA's specialization model. Three types: concept (something to understand), task (something the system does), and reference (lookup information). Each element has a ref attribute pointing to the node being classified and a purpose child explaining why it has that classification.

The relation schema expresses typed, directional semantic links between nodes. Relation types: precedes, depends-on, refines, conflicts-with, implements, constrains, reverses. Each relation has from and to attributes (node IDs). Cross-spec relations use optional to-spec and to-revision attributes to address nodes in other specifications.
spec:clayers.llm:node: The entry point for understanding cognitive layerss. Explains why code alone is insufficient as a source of truth and how a layered specification format addresses this. Start here when orienting to the project.

Describes the layered structure and why layers are separate files. Key insight: separation enables independent versioning, context size control for LLMs, and independent evolution rates. The table lists all layers with their purpose.
